================================================================================
          TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task (MILESTONE TASK — 3 steps)
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor
Milestones: 3 of 3 reviewed

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This task has 3 milestone(s); 0 of 3 reviewed are VULNERABLE.
See per-milestone reviews below for details.

================================================================================
                         PER-MILESTONE REVIEWS
================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 1
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite enforces exact correctness via a ground-truth comparison that
the agent cannot access, and re-runs the Java CLI to verify the pipeline
actually produces the output.

Strengths:  The test re-invokes `com.mariner.forensic.Main rules` at verification
time so the agent cannot bypass the Java pipeline requirement by writing JSON
directly. The final `test_matches_expected_rules` does an exact equality check
against a hardcoded expected file that the agent cannot read.

Weaknesses: None significant — minor redundancy between individual field tests
and the final exact-match test, but this provides clearer diagnostics on
failure without weakening coverage.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

All core constraints for milestone 1 are covered by correctness assertions.
The test suite first verifies the compiled Java entrypoint exists, then
invokes `Main rules` and checks: schema conformance, key-precedence ordering,
nonce-precedence ordering, frm-003 nonce override presence, and finally exact
JSON equality against ground truth in `expected_rules.json`. The ground truth
file lives in `tests/` which is judge-only, so the agent cannot copy it. The
report at `/app/reports/` contains the source data in lengthy appendices
(after ~1900 lines of padding), forcing genuine extraction. No shortcut path
exists because the test calls the Java CLI itself — a pre-written JSON file
at `/app/out/rules.json` would be overwritten by the test harness invocation.

================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 2
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines schema validation, targeted edge-case assertions, and a
full ground-truth comparison that together verify every core constraint from
the milestone instruction.

Strengths:  The tests exercise the two hardest logic paths (rotation replacement
precedence for frm-002 and nonce override for frm-003) with explicit value
checks, then verify the entire output against a hardcoded expected file that
the agent cannot access.

Weaknesses: None significant — the suite is well-layered.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

Milestone 2 requires querying a SQLite audit ledger, applying key-rotation
precedence rules, resolving operative nonces (override before derived), writing
validated JSON, and keeping the Java CLI entrypoint functional. The test suite
covers all of these: it invokes the actual Java program via the CLI entrypoint,
validates the output against the JSON schema, checks frame coverage, tests the
two non-trivial precedence rules with specific value assertions, and finishes
with a full field-by-field comparison to ground truth stored in the test
directory (inaccessible to the agent). No shortcut path exists — the agent
must implement the correlation logic correctly to match the expected output.

================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 3
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite's strongest assertion (`test_matches_expected_findings`)
compares every field of the output against ground-truth values that require
correct AES-256-GCM decryption, making shortcuts infeasible.

Strengths:  The test invokes the decrypt stage via the Java CLI entrypoint and
performs a full field-by-field comparison of all findings against expected
values containing cryptographically-derived plaintext_sha256 hashes that cannot
be guessed without performing actual decryption with correct parameters.

Weaknesses: Config schema validation (policy.yaml and crypto.toml against their
JSON schemas) is not independently asserted — an agent could skip this step
and still pass.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The milestone 3 test suite is well-designed. It runs the Java CLI command
`com.mariner.forensic.Main decrypt`, then checks the output against hardcoded
ground truth in `expected_findings.json`. Because the expected plaintext_sha256
values are SHA-256 hashes of the actual decrypted plaintext, an agent must
correctly parse the GIF, resolve key versions via the exception rules, handle
nonce overrides, and perform AES-256-GCM decryption with frame_id as AAD. The
only gap is that config validation is not independently verified.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Config schema validation not independently tested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   steps/milestone_3/tests/test_m3.py (no assertion exists)
Problem: The instruction says to "validate [policy.yaml and crypto.toml]
against /app/schema/policy.schema.json and /app/schema/crypto.schema.json" but
no test asserts that this validation actually occurs. An agent could skip
config validation entirely and still produce correct findings.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test exists)                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_validates_configs(self) -> None:                                  │
│      """Supplying an invalid config should cause the decrypt stage to       │
│      fail."""                                                               │
│      # e.g., corrupt policy.yaml, invoke decrypt, assert non-zero exit      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a secondary requirement. The core decryption logic is
thoroughly tested via the ground-truth comparison, so the overall suite is
robust. However, a pedantic agent could hard-code the decryption parameters
without reading or validating the config files and still pass.

================================================================================

================================================================================

JSON schemas) is not independently asserted — an agent could skip this step
and still pass.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The milestone 3 test suite is well-designed. It runs the Java CLI command
`com.mariner.forensic.Main decrypt`, then checks the output against hardcoded
ground truth in `expected_findings.json`. Because the expected plaintext_sha256
values are SHA-256 hashes of the actual decrypted plaintext, an agent must
correctly parse the GIF, resolve key versions via the exception rules, handle
nonce overrides, and perform AES-256-GCM decryption with frame_id as AAD. The
only gap is that config validation is not independently verified.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Config schema validation not independently tested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   steps/milestone_3/tests/test_m3.py (no assertion exists)
Problem: The instruction says to "validate [policy.yaml and crypto.toml]
against /app/schema/policy.schema.json and /app/schema/crypto.schema.json" but
no test asserts that this validation actually occurs. An agent could skip
config validation entirely and still produce correct findings.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test exists)                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_validates_configs(self) -> None:                                  │
│      """Supplying an invalid config should cause the decrypt stage to       │
│      fail."""                                                               │
│      # e.g., corrupt policy.yaml, invoke decrypt, assert non-zero exit      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a secondary requirement. The core decryption logic is
thoroughly tested via the ground-truth comparison, so the overall suite is
robust. However, a pedantic agent could hard-code the decryption parameters
without reading or validating the config files and still pass.

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instructions collectively cover what the tests verify. Milestone 1 instruction specifies: the output path (/app/out/rules.json), the required fields (review date, key-selection precedence with 'rotation_replacement' before 'latest_key_assigned', nonce-selection precedence with 'report_override' before 'derived_sha256_prefix', derived-nonce rule in prose, nonce overrides from Appendix D), the schema to validate against, and the 'drop trailing sentence punctuation' detail for `derived_nonce_rule`. Milestone 2 instruction specifies: output path (/app/out/correlation.json), the frames table, key-selection and nonce-selection logic, and schema reference. Milestone 3 instruction specifies: output path (/app/out/findings.json), auth_ok/plaintext_sha256/reason_code fields, schema reference, and that all three payloads should authenticate.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Tests cover the key behaviors specified in each instruction. M1 tests check: Java pipeline entrypoint exists, schema conformance, key precedence order, nonce precedence order, frm-003 nonce override presence and value, and exact match against expected_rules.json. M2 tests check: Java pipeline callable, rules.json still exists, output is array, schema conformance, all frames covered, rotation replacement for frm-002, nonce override for frm-003, and full match against expected_correlation.json. M3 tests check: Java pipeline callable, correlation.json persists, output is array, schema conformance, all frames covered, all three authenticate, reason_code totals, and full match against expected_findings.json. No specified behavior appears untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: All three test files use a clear class-based pytest structure (TestMilestone1Rules, TestMilestone2Correlate, TestMilestone3Decrypt) with docstrings on each test method explaining what is being verified. Constants are named clearly at module level. Helper functions (_class_dir, _run_stage, _load) are well-labeled. Section organization via class and docstrings makes the tests readable and maintainable.
❌ fail - anti_cheating_measures: The expected ground-truth answer files (expected_rules.json, expected_correlation.json, expected_findings.json) are mounted into the verifier container from the tests/ directory and compared with `assert produced == expected`. However, the Dockerfile does NOT copy the steps/ directory (tests/ or solution/) into the image. The .dockerignore also excludes tools/. The agent sees only /app contents. However, the test files themselves contain hardcoded nonce values in assertions (e.g., test_frm003_nonce_override checks `nonce_hex == 'A7C3E91B4D2F8065E1B9C0A3'` directly), and that nonce value is also present in the environment — specifically in the SQLite database (seed.sql stores it in the audit_events table as `nonce_override_hex`) and in policy/config files accessible to the agent. Since the nonce is legitimately in the DB (it's a registered override event the agent must discover), this is by design, not a shortcut leak. The expected JSON files are only in the verifier container (not the agent container), so the agent cannot copy them. Overall anti-cheating is adequate — the agent must derive answers from the report, DB, and GIF.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: All output schemas are explicitly defined in /app/schema/ with JSON Schema files (rules.schema.json, correlation.schema.json, findings.schema.json, policy.schema.json, crypto.schema.json). The instructions reference these schema paths directly. The schemas specify required fields, types, enum values, and regex patterns. Tests also validate against these schemas. This is normative, not merely exemplary.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python dependencies in requirements.txt are fully pinned with exact versions and SHA-256 hashes (generated by uv pip compile --generate-hashes). The requirements.in pins the three direct dependencies to exact versions (pytest==8.2.2, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5, jsonschema==4.23.0). Java dependencies (JAR files) are vendored directly in /app/lib/ with version numbers in filenames. The base Docker image uses a SHA256 digest pin.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. The class path separator in test and solution scripts (:) is correct for Linux. Java class names match consistently across all files. Schema paths, output paths, and frame IDs are consistent throughout.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs lib/, reports/, config/, schema/, fixtures/, and data/seed.sql into the image. The steps/ directory (containing tests/ and solution/) and tools/ directory are not copied into the runtime image. The .dockerignore also explicitly excludes tools/. Tests and solution code remain outside the agent's environment.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: All three solution scripts invoke the compiled Java pipeline (com.mariner.forensic.Main rules/correlate/decrypt) which performs real computation: RuleExtractor parses the ~1900-line forensic report with regex to extract rules; AuditCorrelator queries SQLite with JDBC and applies precedence logic; MediaDecryptor parses the GIF binary, performs AES-256-GCM decryption using keys from the DB, and computes SHA-256 of plaintext. No solution script echoes or cats a hardcoded answer.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: All output file paths are explicitly stated in the instructions: Milestone 1 specifies '/app/out/rules.json', Milestone 2 specifies '/app/out/correlation.json', and Milestone 3 specifies '/app/out/findings.json'. The instructions also explicitly name input files: the report path, policy/config paths, schema paths, JDBC URL, and GIF fixture path.

✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instructions collectively cover what the tests verify. Milestone 2 instruction specifies: output path (/app/out/correlation.json), the frames table, key-selection and nonce-selection logic, and schema reference. Milestone 3 instruction specifies: output path (/app/out/findings.json), auth_ok/plaintext_sha256/reason_code fields, schema reference, and that all three payloads should authenticate.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Tests cover the key behaviors specified in each instruction. M1 tests check: Java pipeline entrypoint exists, schema conformance, key precedence order, nonce precedence order, frm-003 nonce override presence and value, and exact match against expected_rules.json. M2 tests check: Java pipeline callable, rules.json still exists, output is array, schema conformance, all frames covered, rotation replacement for frm-002, nonce override for frm-003, and full match against expected_correlation.json. M3 tests check: Java pipeline callable, correlation.json persists, output is array, schema conformance, all frames covered, all three authenticate, reason_code totals, and full match against expected_findings.json. No specified behavior appears untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: All three test files use a clear class-based pytest structure (TestMilestone1Rules, TestMilestone2Correlate, TestMilestone3Decrypt) with docstrings on each test method explaining what is being verified. Section organization via class and docstrings makes the tests readable and maintainable.
❌ fail - anti_cheating_measures: The expected ground-truth answer files (expected_rules.json, expected_correlation.json, expected_findings.json) are mounted into the verifier container from the tests/ directory and compared with `assert produced == expected`. However, the Dockerfile does NOT copy the steps/ directory (tests/ or solution/) into the image. The .dockerignore also excludes tools/. The agent sees only /app contents. However, the test files themselves contain hardcoded nonce values in assertions (e.g., test_frm003_nonce_override checks `nonce_hex == 'A7C3E91B4D2F8065E1B9C0A3'` directly), and that nonce value is also present in the environment — specifically in the SQLite database (seed.sql stores it in the audit_events table as `nonce_override_hex`) and in policy/config files accessible to the agent. Since the nonce is legitimately in the DB (it's a registered override event the agent must discover), this is by design, not a shortcut leak. The expected JSON files are only in the verifier container (not the agent container), so the agent cannot copy them. Overall anti-cheating is adequate — the agent must derive answers from the report, DB, and GIF.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: All output schemas are explicitly defined in /app/schema/ with JSON Schema files (rules.schema.json, correlation.schema.json, findings.schema.json, policy.schema.json, crypto.schema.json). ✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. The class path separator in test and solution scripts (:) is correct for Linux. Java class names match consistently across all files. Schema paths, output paths, and frame IDs are consistent throughout.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs lib/, reports/, config/, schema/, fixtures/, and data/seed.sql into the image. The steps/ directory (containing tests/ and solution/) and tools/ directory are not copied into the runtime image. The .dockerignore also explicitly excludes tools/. Tests and solution code remain outside the agent's environment.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: All three solution scripts invoke the compiled Java pipeline (com.mariner.forensic.Main rules/correlate/decrypt) which performs real computation: RuleExtractor parses the ~1900-line forensic report with regex to extract rules; AuditCorrelator queries SQLite with JDBC and applies precedence logic; MediaDecryptor parses the GIF binary, performs AES-256-GCM decryption using keys from the DB, and computes SHA-256 of plaintext. Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ✅ PASS, ## Job Run Summary

### 1. Overall Results
- **Trials:** 1 total — 0 fully passed, 1 partially passed
- **Trial:** `tbench-task__hienZft` — reward **0.333** (1/3 milestones)
- Only one agent/model was used; no cross-agent comparison is possible.

### 2. Common Failure Patterns
With a single trial, patterns are limited to this run, but two distinct failure modes appeared:
- **Terminal output desync:** The agent misread stale/desynced terminal output and falsely believed Milestone 2 (SQLite correlation) had succeeded. This caused it to proceed without actually implementing the `correlate` stage, which the verifier confirmed was still emitting "not yet implemented."
- **Cascading failure:** Milestone 3's decryption logic appeared correct (valid `findings.json` produced, all 3 frames authenticated), but it was penalized solely because `correlation.json` was never written — `test_milestone2_artifact_persists` failed as a downstream consequence of the M2 failure.

### 3. Hack Check
**No hacking detected.** The agent operated entirely within the task environment (writing/compiling Java, querying the SQLite DB, processing the GIF fixture). No reads from solution files, no writes to `reward.txt`/`reward.json`, no test file modification. The M2 failure was a genuine implementation defect.

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues
**None found.** Both checks marked `task_specification: pass`. All three milestones provided sufficient detail (field names, schema references, key-precedence rules, cryptographic identifiers). Failures were execution-side, not specification-side.

One environmental concern worth noting: a **`max_tokens` truncation** occurred during Milestone 2, which may have contributed to the agent losing context and failing to detect the desync. This is a harness/resource issue rather than an instruction gap.

### 5. Progress on Failed Milestones
| Milestone | Result | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| M1 (rules.json extraction) | ✅ 1.0 | All 6 tests passed |
| M2 (SQLite correlation.json) | ❌ 0.0 | Not implemented; agent believed it had succeeded |
| M3 (AES-GCM findings.json) | ❌ 0.0 (penalized) | Implementation likely correct; failed only on M2 artifact dependency |

The agent was effectively **2/3 of the way complete** in terms of implementation quality — M3 was functionally working. True earned progress is closer to **~67%** despite the 33% reward score.

### 6. Key Differences Between Agents/Models
Not applicable — only one trial was run. A follow-up run with the same agent should focus on resolving the terminal desync issue (e.g., explicit output file verification rather than relying on terminal stdout) and ensuring adequate token budget for the full session.

                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is a multi-step (3 milestones) security/cryptography task requiring agents
to build a Java CLI pipeline that: (1) extracts cryptographic exception rules
from a ~1900-line forensic report, (2) correlates audit events from a SQLite
database applying key/nonce precedence rules, and (3) decrypts AES-256-GCM
payloads embedded in GIF application extension blocks. The solution implements a
well-structured Java pipeline compiled against vendored JAR libraries. Tests
validate JSON schema conformance and exact match against ground-truth expected
outputs for each milestone.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Non-Canonical Base Image
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim-bookworm base image, but the
         task's primary language is Java (with bash and SQL). A Python base
         image is used solely because test dependencies (pytest, jsonschema)
         are Python packages. The canonical base image list should be checked
         for a more appropriate Java-oriented base, or this choice should be
         explicitly justified.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:01f4  │
│  2367a0a94ad4bc17111776fd66e3500c1d87c15bbd6055b7371d39c124fb              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The task is a Java+SQLite task that happens to need Python only for
the test runner (pytest + jsonschema). The Python slim-bookworm image with a
digest pin is reasonable given that both the solution (Java) and the verifier
(Python/pytest) need their runtimes. However, this should be confirmed against
the canonical base image list — if a dedicated Python 3.13 slim image is the
canonical one for Python-based verifiers, this is acceptable. The digest pin is
correct practice.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Missing task.toml Fields: [verifier] and [agent] at Root Level
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (lines 16-49)
Problem: The task.toml defines per-step `[steps.agent]` and `[steps.verifier]`
         sections but does not include root-level `[verifier]` or `[agent]`
         sections with `timeout_sec`. While the Harbor multi-step framework
         reads per-step timeouts from `[[steps]]` entries, the Terminus 2.0
         schema specifies root-level `[verifier]` and `[agent]` as required.

Current approach: Per-step timeouts are defined inline under each `[[steps]]`
entry (agent: 1800s, verifier: 450s).

Suggested fix: Add root-level sections for compatibility with tooling that
expects the standard schema:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  [verifier]                                                                 │
│  timeout_sec = 450.0                                                        │
│                                                                             │
│  [agent]                                                                    │
│  timeout_sec = 1800.0                                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Some validation tooling and the standard task.toml schema expect
these root-level sections to exist even when per-step overrides are present.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Task Directory Name Not Descriptive
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)

Current approach: The task directory is named "tbench-task" which is a generic
placeholder rather than a descriptive kebab-case name.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  mariner-aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: A descriptive name like "mariner-aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline" or
"aes-gcm-media-evidence-review" would immediately convey the task's domain and
make it identifiable in a task registry.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Tags Array Has Only 5 Items (Minor)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 13)
Problem: The tags array has 5 items which is valid (3-6 required), but could
         benefit from a "forensics" or "cryptography" tag to better describe
         the domain.

Current approach: `tags = ["security", "aes-gcm", "java", "sqlite", "jdbc"]`

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tags = ["security", "cryptography", "aes-gcm", "java", "sqlite", "jdbc"]  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: Adding "cryptography" as a tag improves discoverability and more
accurately captures the task's primary domain focus.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, challenging multi-step security task that effectively
tests an agent's ability to parse dense long-context documents, interact with
SQLite databases via JDBC, implement cryptographic operations in Java, and
produce schema-validated JSON outputs. The task structure is clean and follows
Harbor multi-step conventions correctly.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent multi-step progression with logical dependency chain
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: 1900-line report with intentional noise, scrambled
    DB insert order, and crypto operations that require genuine computation
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with schema validation and exact ground-truth
    matching across all three milestones
  ✓ All dependencies vendored offline (JAR files, pip packages with hashes)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Missing root-level [verifier] and [agent] sections in task.toml
  ✗ Generic directory name ("tbench-task") rather than descriptive kebab-case

Evaluates: long-context comprehension, Java development, SQLite/JDBC
           interaction, AES-GCM cryptography, JSON schema compliance

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  The task is high quality and nearly ready. Add root-level [verifier] and
  [agent] timeout sections to task.toml for schema compliance, and rename
  the directory to something descriptive before submission.

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is a multi-step (3 milestones) security/cryptography task requiring agents
to build a Java CLI pipeline that: (1) extracts cryptographic exception rules
from a ~1900-line forensic report, (2) correlates audit events from a SQLite
database applying key/nonce precedence rules, and (3) decrypts AES-256-GCM
payloads embedded in GIF application extension blocks. The solution implements a
well-structured Java pipeline compiled against vendored JAR libraries. Tests
validate JSON schema conformance and exact match against ground-truth expected
outputs for each milestone.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Non-Canonical Base Image
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim-bookworm base image, but the
         task's primary language is Java (with bash and SQL). A Python base
         image is used solely because test dependencies (pytest, jsonschema)
         are Python packages. The canonical base image list should be checked
         for a more appropriate Java-oriented base, or this choice should be
         explicitly justified.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:01f4  │
│  2367a0a94ad4bc17111776fd66e3500c1d87c15bbd6055b7371d39c124fb              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The task is a Java+SQLite task that happens to need Python only for
the test runner (pytest + jsonschema). The Python slim-bookworm image with a
digest pin is reasonable given that both the solution (Java) and the verifier
(Python/pytest) need their runtimes. However, this should be confirmed against
the canonical base image list — if a dedicated Python 3.13 slim image is the
canonical one for Python-based verifiers, this is acceptable. The digest pin is
correct practice.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Missing task.toml Fields: [verifier] and [agent] at Root Level
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (lines 16-49)
Problem: The task.toml defines per-step `[steps.agent]` and `[steps.verifier]`
         sections but does not include root-level `[verifier]` or `[agent]`
         sections with `timeout_sec`. While the Harbor multi-step framework
         reads per-step timeouts from `[[steps]]` entries, the Terminus 2.0
         schema specifies root-level `[verifier]` and `[agent]` as required.

Current approach: Per-step timeouts are defined inline under each `[[steps]]`
entry (agent: 1800s, verifier: 450s).

Suggested fix: Add root-level sections for compatibility with tooling that
expects the standard schema:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  [verifier]                                                                 │
│  timeout_sec = 450.0                                                        │
│                                                                             │
│  [agent]                                                                    │
│  timeout_sec = 1800.0                                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Some validation tooling and the standard task.toml schema expect
these root-level sections to exist even when per-step overrides are present.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Task Directory Name Not Descriptive
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)

Current approach: The task directory is named "tbench-task" which is a generic
placeholder rather than a descriptive kebab-case name.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  mariner-aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: A descriptive name like "mariner-aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline" or
"aes-gcm-media-evidence-review" would immediately convey the task's domain and
make it identifiable in a task registry.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Tags Array Has Only 5 Items (Minor)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 13)
Problem: The tags array has 5 items which is valid (3-6 required), but could
         benefit from a "forensics" or "cryptography" tag to better describe
         the domain.

Current approach: `tags = ["security", "aes-gcm", "java", "sqlite", "jdbc"]`

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tags = ["security", "cryptography", "aes-gcm", "java", "sqlite", "jdbc"]  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: Adding "cryptography" as a tag improves discoverability and more
accurately captures the task's primary domain focus.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, challenging multi-step security task that effectively
tests an agent's ability to parse dense long-context documents, interact with
SQLite databases via JDBC, implement cryptographic operations in Java, and
produce schema-validated JSON outputs. The task structure is clean and follows
Harbor multi-step conventions correctly.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent multi-step progression with logical dependency chain
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: 1900-line report with intentional noise, scrambled
    DB insert order, and crypto operations that require genuine computation
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with schema validation and exact ground-truth
    matching across all three milestones
  ✓ All dependencies vendored offline (JAR files, pip packages with hashes)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Missing root-level [verifier] and [agent] sections in task.toml
  ✗ Generic directory name ("tbench-task") rather than descriptive kebab-case

Evaluates: long-context comprehension, Java development, SQLite/JDBC
           interaction, AES-GCM cryptography, JSON schema compliance

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  The task is high quality and nearly ready. Add root-level [verifier] and
  [agent] timeout sections to task.toml for schema compliance, and rename
  the directory to something descriptive before submission.
================================================================================
output_data.rubric:
# Rubric 1
Agent reads the forensic report (especially appendices) before attempting to generate rules.json, +2
Agent reads /app/schema/rules.schema.json to understand the expected output structure (nonce_overrides is an object keyed by frame_id, not an array), +2
Agent produces /app/out/rules.json with correct key_selection_precedence ordering (rotation_replacement before latest_key_assigned), +3
Agent extracts the frm-003 nonce override from Appendix D and includes it in nonce_overrides, +5
Agent extracts a concise derived_nonce_rule without trailing punctuation (e.g. matching the expected format rather than copying an entire paragraph), +3
Agent compiles the Java pipeline so com.mariner.forensic.Main rules is callable from /app/pipeline/classes or /app/classes, +3
Agent validates the output against rules.schema.json or at least inspects the schema to check conformance, +1
Agent copies an overly long or incorrect prose string into derived_nonce_rule instead of the concise rule, -3

# Rubric 2
Agent queries the SQLite database to inspect audit_events and frames tables before implementing correlation logic, +2
Agent correctly implements key-selection precedence: key_rotated replacement_key_version wins over later key_assigned rows, +5
Agent resolves the nonce for frm-003 as an override (from rules.json nonce_overrides) rather than deriving it, +3
Agent computes derived nonces as SHA-256(frame_id + ':' + key_version) truncated to first 12 bytes in uppercase hex, +3
Agent produces /app/out/correlation.json conforming to correlation.schema.json with all three in-scope frames, +3
Agent keeps the correlate stage callable as com.mariner.forensic.Main correlate from previously compiled classes, +2
Agent fails to read or use rules.json from milestone 1 when resolving nonce overrides, -3

# Rubric 3
Agent parses the GIF fixture to extract per-frame ciphertext from MRNR/CRYPTO1 application extension blocks, +3
Agent loads key material from SQLite key_material table using the key_version from correlation.json, +3
Agent uses AES-256-GCM with 128-bit authentication tag, the resolved nonce, and frame_id as additional authenticated data (AAD), +5
Agent produces /app/out/findings.json with all three frames showing auth_ok=true when exception rules are correctly followed, +5
Agent validates config files (policy.yaml and crypto.toml) against their respective schemas before decryption, +1
Agent keeps the decrypt stage callable as com.mariner.forensic.Main decrypt from compiled classes, +2
Agent produces findings where some frames fail authentication due to incorrect key/nonce resolution, -3
Agent hardcodes decryption results or plaintext_sha256 values instead of performing actual AES-GCM decryption, -5

                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is a multi-step (3 milestones) security/cryptography task requiring agents
to build a Java CLI pipeline that: (1) extracts cryptographic exception rules
from a ~1900-line forensic report, (2) correlates audit events from a SQLite
database applying key/nonce precedence rules, and (3) decrypts AES-256-GCM
payloads embedded in GIF application extension blocks. The solution implements a
well-structured Java pipeline compiled against vendored JAR libraries. Tests
validate JSON schema conformance and exact match against ground-truth expected
outputs for each milestone.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Non-Canonical Base Image
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim-bookworm base image, but the
         task's primary language is Java (with bash and SQL). A Python base
         image is used solely because test dependencies (pytest, jsonschema)
         are Python packages. The canonical base image list should be checked
         for a more appropriate Java-oriented base, or this choice should be
         explicitly justified.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:01f4  │
│  2367a0a94ad4bc17111776fd66e3500c1d87c15bbd6055b7371d39c124fb              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The task is a Java+SQLite task that happens to need Python only for
the test runner (pytest + jsonschema). The Python slim-bookworm image with a
digest pin is reasonable given that both the solution (Java) and the verifier
(Python/pytest) need their runtimes. However, this should be confirmed against
the canonical base image list — if a dedicated Python 3.13 slim image is the
canonical one for Python-based verifiers, this is acceptable. The digest pin is
correct practice.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Missing task.toml Fields: [verifier] and [agent] at Root Level
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (lines 16-49)
Problem: The task.toml defines per-step `[steps.agent]` and `[steps.verifier]`
         sections but does not include root-level `[verifier]` or `[agent]`
         sections with `timeout_sec`. While the Harbor multi-step framework
         reads per-step timeouts from `[[steps]]` entries, the Terminus 2.0
         schema specifies root-level `[verifier]` and `[agent]` as required.

Current approach: Per-step timeouts are defined inline under each `[[steps]]`
entry (agent: 1800s, verifier: 450s).

Suggested fix: Add root-level sections for compatibility with tooling that
expects the standard schema:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  [verifier]                                                                 │
│  timeout_sec = 450.0                                                        │
│                                                                             │
│  [agent]                                                                    │
│  timeout_sec = 1800.0                                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Some validation tooling and the standard task.toml schema expect
these root-level sections to exist even when per-step overrides are present.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Task Directory Name Not Descriptive
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)

Current approach: The task directory is named "tbench-task" which is a generic
placeholder rather than a descriptive kebab-case name.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  mariner-aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: A descriptive name like "mariner-aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline" or
"aes-gcm-media-evidence-review" would immediately convey the task's domain and
make it identifiable in a task registry.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Tags Array Has Only 5 Items (Minor)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 13)
Problem: The tags array has 5 items which is valid (3-6 required), but could
         benefit from a "forensics" or "cryptography" tag to better describe
         the domain.

Current approach: `tags = ["security", "aes-gcm", "java", "sqlite", "jdbc"]`

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tags = ["security", "cryptography", "aes-gcm", "java", "sqlite", "jdbc"]  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: Adding "cryptography" as a tag improves discoverability and more
accurately captures the task's primary domain focus.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, challenging multi-step security task that effectively
tests an agent's ability to parse dense long-context documents, interact with
SQLite databases via JDBC, implement cryptographic operations in Java, and
produce schema-validated JSON outputs. The task structure is clean and follows
Harbor multi-step conventions correctly.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent multi-step progression with logical dependency chain
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: 1900-line report with intentional noise, scrambled
    DB insert order, and crypto operations that require genuine computation
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with schema validation and exact ground-truth
    matching across all three milestones
  ✓ All dependencies vendored offline (JAR files, pip packages with hashes)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Missing root-level [verifier] and [agent] sections in task.toml
  ✗ Generic directory name ("tbench-task") rather than descriptive kebab-case

Evaluates: long-context comprehension, Java development, SQLite/JDBC
           interaction, AES-GCM cryptography, JSON schema compliance

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  The task is high quality and nearly ready. Add root-level [verifier] and
  [agent] timeout sections to task.toml for schema compliance, and rename
  the directory to something descriptive before submission.

                         PER-MILESTONE REVIEWS

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 1
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite verifies full correctness of the extracted rules via deep
equality against ground truth that the agent cannot access, and enforces
the Java CLI pipeline constraint structurally.

Strengths:  The final `test_matches_expected_rules` performs a full deep-equality
check against hidden ground truth in tests/expected_rules.json, which the agent
cannot read. Earlier tests provide incremental diagnostics for partial failures.

Weaknesses: No test verifies that the Java program actually parses the report
file (vs. emitting hardcoded JSON), but this is a minor stylistic concern since
the agent still must read the report to discover the correct values and must
build compilable Java with the correct classpath.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The milestone 1 test suite contains six tests covering the Java entrypoint
existence, JSON schema conformance, ordering constraints on two precedence
arrays, the frm-003 nonce override, and a full deep-equality comparison
against expected output. The deep-equality check is the strongest guard:
it compares every field value against a hidden reference file the agent
cannot access. The agent must read the 1900-line report to find the
appendix data, write and compile Java code under com.mariner.forensic.Main,
and produce schema-valid JSON — there is no trivial shortcut that avoids
this work since the answer values are buried in appendices and the expected
output file lives in tests/ (inaccessible to the agent).

================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 2
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite thoroughly validates milestone 2's correlation logic through both
targeted assertions on tricky edge cases and a comprehensive field-by-field
comparison against hardcoded expected output that includes cryptographically
derived values the agent cannot guess.

Strengths:  The tests invoke the Java CLI entrypoint directly, validate schema
conformance, check specific precedence edge cases (rotation > assignment for
frm-002, nonce override for frm-003), and perform a full equality check against
ground truth that includes SHA-256-derived nonce hex values.

Weaknesses: The dataset is small (3 frames, 6 events), which reduces confidence
that the implementation generalizes, but this does not constitute a vulnerability
since the agent cannot read the expected output.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

Milestone 2 requires querying SQLite audit events, applying key-selection
and nonce-selection precedence rules, computing SHA-256-derived nonces, and
writing a correlation JSON file via a Java CLI entrypoint. The test suite
covers every extracted constraint with correctness assertions. The final
test (`test_matches_expected_correlation`) compares all fields of all rows
against hardcoded ground truth containing SHA-256-derived nonce hex values
that cannot be produced without running the actual algorithm. The Java
entrypoint is exercised directly by the test fixture, and the expected
output file resides in tests/ which the agent cannot access.

================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 3
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite enforces exact field-by-field correctness of the decryption
output via hardcoded ground truth that the agent cannot access, and validates
the Java CLI entrypoint requirement by actually invoking it.

Strengths:  The tests verify the full decryption pipeline end-to-end by
comparing every output field (including plaintext_sha256 hashes that can only
be produced through correct AES-256-GCM decryption) against ground truth
stored exclusively in the verifier container.

Weaknesses: Config schema validation (policy.yaml, crypto.toml) is only
tested implicitly through successful decryption output rather than with a
dedicated assertion that validation occurred at runtime.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The milestone 3 test suite comprises eight test methods that collectively
verify: (1) the Java CLI entrypoint exists and is callable as
`com.mariner.forensic.Main decrypt`, (2) the output conforms to the
findings JSON schema, (3) exactly three frames are covered, (4) all three
authenticate successfully, and (5) every field in every frame matches
hardcoded expected values including cryptographic plaintext_sha256 hashes.
The plaintext hashes serve as a strong correctness oracle — they can only
be produced by correctly extracting ciphertext from the GIF, resolving
key/nonce precedence from the audit DB, and performing real AES-256-GCM
decryption with proper AAD binding. No shortcut path exists.

================================================================================

================================================================================

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: All three milestone instructions explicitly describe what the tests verify. M1 covers extracting rules from report appendices into /app/out/rules.json (review_date, key/nonce precedence, derived_nonce_rule, nonce_overrides) validated against rules.schema.json. M2 covers querying SQLite by recorded_at order, applying precedence rules, writing /app/out/correlation.json validated against correlation.schema.json. M3 covers parsing MRNR/CRYPTO1 GIF blocks, AES-256-GCM decryption with frame_id as AAD, and writing /app/out/findings.json with auth_ok, plaintext_sha256, reason_code. The Java CLI interface (com.mariner.forensic.Main rules/correlate/decrypt) is also explicitly specified.
❌ fail - behavior_in_tests: M3 instruction says to load and validate policy.yaml against policy.schema.json and crypto.toml against crypto.schema.json, but no test verifies this validation step. The tests only check that findings.json is produced correctly; whether config validation was performed is not tested. This means an implementation that skips config validation and goes straight to decryption would still pass all tests.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: All three test files are well-structured: each has a class-level docstring, individual test methods with descriptive names and docstrings, and logical grouping by concern (schema conformance, frame coverage, precedence checks, full match). Fixtures are cleanly separated. The structure is readable and maintainable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the runtime image. The expected_*.json files with ground-truth answers (nonces, SHA-256 hashes) live only in the verifier image. The key hex values are SHA-256 hashes of internal strings seeded in the SQLite DB, not guessable without the DB. The nonce override is embedded in both the report and the DB, which is correct (the agent must extract it from those sources). internet access is disabled. The agent cannot shortcut to the expected files.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: Each output file (rules.json, correlation.json, findings.json) has a corresponding JSON Schema under /app/schema/ that is explicitly referenced by name in the instructions. The schemas use additionalProperties:false with required fields, enum constraints, and pattern constraints — making them fully normative specifications, not just examples.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python dependencies in requirements.txt are fully pinned with exact versions and SHA-256 hashes (--require-hashes). Java JARs are vendored in /app/lib/ with version numbers in filenames (e.g., jackson-databind-2.17.2.jar, sqlite-jdbc-3.46.1.0.jar). apt packages are not version-pinned, which is acceptable per the criteria.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. All cross-references are consistent: output paths (/app/out/rules.json, /app/out/correlation.json, /app/out/findings.json), class names (com.mariner.forensic.Main), CLI verbs (rules, correlate, decrypt), schema references, and JDBC URL all match across instructions, tests, solution scripts, and environment configuration.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies lib/, reports/, config/, schema/, fixtures/, and the SQL seed file into the runtime image. Neither the tests/ nor solution/ directories are included. The .dockerignore also excludes tools/.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution scripts compile a Java pipeline from source and invoke com.mariner.forensic.Main with the appropriate stage argument. The Java source genuinely parses the forensic report via regex for rules, queries SQLite for audit events and resolves precedence, and decrypts GIF payloads with AES-GCM. No answers are hardcoded or echoed directly.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: All three output filenames are explicitly stated in their respective instructions: M1 says '/app/out/rules.json', M2 says '/app/out/correlation.json', M3 says '/app/out/findings.json'. Input files and their paths are also explicitly provided.

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is a hard, 3-milestone security task requiring agents to build a Java CLI
pipeline that extracts cryptographic exception rules from a long forensic
report (milestone 1), correlates audit events from SQLite using those rules
(milestone 2), and decrypts AES-256-GCM payloads embedded in GIF application
extension blocks (milestone 3). The solution implements a well-structured Java
pipeline with Main entrypoint dispatching to RuleExtractor, AuditCorrelator,
and MediaDecryptor stages. Tests verify schema conformance, precedence logic,
and full ground-truth match for each stage's JSON output.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Non-Canonical Docker Base Image
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (line 1)
Problem: The Dockerfile uses the Python 3.13 slim-bookworm base image, but the
         task's primary language is Java (openjdk-17). Using a Python base for
         a Java task is unusual — the canonical base image list likely has a
         more appropriate Java base, and Python is only needed as a test runner
         (pytest), not as the task's primary runtime.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FROM public.ecr.aws/docker/library/python:3.13-slim-bookworm@sha256:...   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: While this technically works (Java is installed via apt-get and
Python is needed for pytest), a canonical Java base with Python added for
testing would better match the task's primary language. However, since the
task requires both Java (for the pipeline) and Python (for jsonschema
validation in tests and pytest), this dual-language need may justify the
Python base. Verify against the canonical base image list.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Task Directory Name Not Descriptive
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)
Problem: The directory is named "tbench-task" which is a generic placeholder
         rather than a descriptive kebab-case task identifier. A name like
         "aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline" or "mariner-crypto-audit" would better
         identify the task's purpose.

Current approach: Generic directory name "tbench-task"

Suggested fix: Rename to a descriptive kebab-case name such as
"mariner-aes-gcm-forensic-pipeline" or "gif-crypto-evidence-review"

Explanation: Descriptive task names improve discoverability and make it
easier to identify tasks in the benchmark suite.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. test.sh Missing Working Directory Check
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/steps/milestone_1/tests/test.sh (and milestone_2, _3)

Current approach: The test.sh scripts jump directly into running pytest
without checking the working directory first.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/bin/bash                                                                │
│  set -uo pipefail                                                           │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ "$PWD" = "/" ]; then                                                  │
│      echo "Error: No working directory set."                                │
│      exit 1                                                                 │
│  fi                                                                         │
│                                                                             │
│  mkdir -p /logs/verifier                                                    │
│  ...                                                                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: Adding the working directory guard is a recommended practice
that catches misconfigured containers early with a clear error message.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an exceptionally well-crafted hard-difficulty task that exercises deep
reasoning across long-context reading, SQL correlation logic, binary format
parsing, and applied cryptography. The multi-step architecture is correctly
structured and the anti-cheating properties are strong.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent long-context challenge — 1900-line report with deliberately
    repetitive filler that hides the decisive rules in appendices
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: keys in DB, ciphertext in binary GIF, rules buried
    in prose — no shortcut bypasses the intended reasoning pipeline
  ✓ Comprehensive test suites with schema validation, individual field
    checks, and full ground-truth comparison per milestone
  ✓ Well-structured multi-step layout with proper task.toml [[steps]],
    per-step instructions, solutions, and tests

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Generic task directory name ("tbench-task") instead of descriptive ID
  ✗ Base image choice could be more canonical for a Java-primary task

Evaluates: long-context comprehension, Java build/classpath management,
           SQL temporal reasoning, AES-GCM cryptographic implementation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is well-designed with correct multi-step structure, strong test
  coverage, and effective anti-cheating measures. The warnings are cosmetic
  and do not affect task correctness or agent evaluation quality.

          TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task (MILESTONE TASK — 3 steps)
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None
Milestones: 3 of 3 reviewed

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This task has 3 milestone(s); 0 of 3 reviewed are VULNERABLE.
See per-milestone reviews below for details.

================================================================================
                         PER-MILESTONE REVIEWS
================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 1
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite verifies full correctness of the extracted rules via deep
equality against ground truth that the agent cannot access, and enforces
the Java CLI pipeline constraint structurally.

Strengths:  The final `test_matches_expected_rules` performs a full deep-equality
check against hidden ground truth in tests/expected_rules.json, which the agent
cannot read. Earlier tests provide incremental diagnostics for partial failures.

Weaknesses: No test verifies that the Java program actually parses the report
file (vs. emitting hardcoded JSON), but this is a minor stylistic concern since
the agent still must read the report to discover the correct values and must
build compilable Java with the correct classpath.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The milestone 1 test suite contains six tests covering the Java entrypoint
existence, JSON schema conformance, ordering constraints on two precedence
arrays, the frm-003 nonce override, and a full deep-equality comparison
against expected output. The deep-equality check is the strongest guard:
it compares every field value against a hidden reference file the agent
cannot access. The agent must read the 1900-line report to find the
appendix data, write and compile Java code under com.mariner.forensic.Main,
and produce schema-valid JSON — there is no trivial shortcut that avoids
this work since the answer values are buried in appendices and the expected
output file lives in tests/ (inaccessible to the agent).

================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 2
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite thoroughly validates milestone 2's correlation logic through both
targeted assertions on tricky edge cases and a comprehensive field-by-field
comparison against hardcoded expected output that includes cryptographically
derived values the agent cannot guess.

Strengths:  The tests invoke the Java CLI entrypoint directly, validate schema
conformance, check specific precedence edge cases (rotation > assignment for
frm-002, nonce override for frm-003), and perform a full equality check against
ground truth that includes SHA-256-derived nonce hex values.

Weaknesses: The dataset is small (3 frames, 6 events), which reduces confidence
that the implementation generalizes, but this does not constitute a vulnerability
since the agent cannot read the expected output.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

Milestone 2 requires querying SQLite audit events, applying key-selection
and nonce-selection precedence rules, computing SHA-256-derived nonces, and
writing a correlation JSON file via a Java CLI entrypoint. The test suite
covers every extracted constraint with correctness assertions. The final
test (`test_matches_expected_correlation`) compares all fields of all rows
against hardcoded ground truth containing SHA-256-derived nonce hex values
that cannot be produced without running the actual algorithm. The Java
entrypoint is exercised directly by the test fixture, and the expected
output file resides in tests/ which the agent cannot access.

================================================================================

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task milestone 3
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite enforces exact field-by-field correctness of the decryption
output via hardcoded ground truth that the agent cannot access, and validates
the Java CLI entrypoint requirement by actually invoking it.

Strengths:  The tests verify the full decryption pipeline end-to-end by
comparing every output field (including plaintext_sha256 hashes that can only
be produced through correct AES-256-GCM decryption) against ground truth
stored exclusively in the verifier container.

Weaknesses: Config schema validation (policy.yaml, crypto.toml) is only
tested implicitly through successful decryption output rather than with a
dedicated assertion that validation occurred at runtime.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The milestone 3 test suite comprises eight test methods that collectively
verify: (1) the Java CLI entrypoint exists and is callable as
`com.mariner.forensic.Main decrypt`, (2) the output conforms to the
findings JSON schema, (3) exactly three frames are covered, (4) all three
authenticate successfully, and (5) every field in every frame matches
hardcoded expected values including cryptographic plaintext_sha256 hashes.
The plaintext hashes serve as a strong correctness oracle — they can only
be produced by correctly extracting ciphertext from the GIF, resolving
key/nonce precedence from the audit DB, and performing real AES-256-GCM
decryption with proper AAD binding. No shortcut path exists.

================================================================================

================================================================================
